programs.name: Healthy Connections Medicaid
programs.what_it_does: Provides medical coverage for hospital care, health center services, doctor care, nurse care, lab and X-ray services, and nursing homes for eligible seniors.[3] It also supports long-term care in nursing homes, assisted living, adult foster care, and home-based services for frail seniors.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors aged 65+, blind or disabled individuals with low income and assets (e.g., single applicant income under $2,982/month, assets under $2,000 in 2026).[2][3]
programs.benefit_value: Full Medicaid-covered services including long-term care; income/asset limits vary by program and family size (e.g., $1,330/month for one at 100% FPL).[6]

programs.name: Community Choices Waiver
programs.what_it_does: Provides home and community-based services for individuals requiring nursing home level of care but wishing to live at home, in adult foster care, or community residential care facilities.[2] Benefits include home modifications, adult day health care, specialized medical equipment, and assistance with ADLs like bathing, mobility, and eating.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Elderly and disabled individuals needing nursing home level of care, meeting financial eligibility (e.g., income under $2,982/month, assets under $2,000 for singles).[2]
programs.benefit_value: Non-medical supports to avoid institutionalization; specific value varies by needs and eligibility.

programs.name: South Carolina PACE
programs.what_it_does: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE) delivers comprehensive medical and social services to eligible frail seniors in community settings, including adult day health care and transportation. It coordinates all Medicare and Medicaid benefits for participants.
programs.who_its_for: Frail seniors aged 55+ certified to need nursing home level care, able to live safely in the community, with Medicare and Medicaid eligibility.
programs.benefit_value: Capitated full-risk care model; covers all needed services without copays for dual eligibles.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.what_it_does: Pays for Medicare Part B premiums, deductibles, and copayments based on income and assets.[3] QMB covers full Part B premium and more; SLMB and QI cover premiums only.[7]
programs.who_its_for: Medicare beneficiaries with low income (e.g., around $1,226/month for one person).[3]
programs.benefit_value: Varies by program: full premium coverage (~$185/month Part B in 2026) plus cost-sharing for QMB; amounts depend on income/assets.[3]

programs.name: SNAP (Food Assistance)
programs.what_it_does: Provides monthly benefits via EBT card to buy food for nutritious meals. Administered statewide through SC Department of Social Services.
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors (60+) with income typically under 130-200% FPL, assets under $4,500 (higher for elderly).
programs.benefit_value: Average $200-300/month per senior household; max benefits ~$291 for one in 2026.

programs.name: LIHEAP (Home Energy Assistance)
programs.what_it_does: Offers financial assistance for heating and cooling bills, crisis intervention, and weather-related repairs. Administered through community action agencies in SC.
programs.who_its_for: Low-income households including seniors 60+, priority for disabled/elderly, income <150% FPL.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program
programs.what_it_does: Insulates homes, seals leaks, and improves energy efficiency through free upgrades like attic insulation and furnace repairs. Administered via SC Community Action Partnerships.
programs.who_its_for: Low-income homeowners/renters, priority for seniors 60+, disabled, income <200% FPL.
programs.benefit_value: Free services valued at $5,000-10,000 per home in energy savings over time.

programs.name: SHIP (I-Care)
programs.what_it_does: Provides free one-on-one counseling on Medicare, Medicaid, insurance choices, and assistance with applications.[4][8] Helps beneficiaries understand options and save money.
programs.who_its_for: Medicare beneficiaries, families, caregivers, and those applying for Medicare/Medicaid.[4]
programs.benefit_value: Free counseling; potential savings of thousands annually on premiums/drugs.

programs.name: Meals on Wheels
programs.what_it_does: Delivers nutritious meals to homebound seniors to promote health and independence. Often funded via Older Americans Act through SC aging councils.
programs.who_its_for: Homebound seniors 60+ unable to prepare meals, often low-income or at nutrition risk.

programs.name: Caregiver/Respite Support
programs.what_it_does: Offers respite for family caregivers through temporary relief services like in-home aid or adult day care. Provided via SC Department on Aging and waivers.
programs.who_its_for: Family caregivers of seniors/disabled needing break from care duties.
programs.benefit_value: Varies; grants up to $1,000-2,000/year or hourly respite services.

programs.name: SCSEP (Senior Community Service Employment Program)
programs.what_it_does: Provides part-time paid community service jobs with training to transition to unsubsidized employment. Administered through SC Department on Aging.
programs.who_its_for: Unemployed low-income seniors 55+ unable to find work.
programs.benefit_value: Minimum wage jobs ~20 hours/week, ~$7,000/year training support.

programs.name: Legal Aid for Seniors
programs.what_it_does: Offers free legal assistance for issues like housing, benefits, abuse via SC Legal Services and Aging programs. Helps navigate rights and disputes.
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors 60+ facing legal barriers to services.
programs.benefit_value: Free representation; resolves cases worth thousands in benefits/housing.

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.what_it_does: Advocates for residents in long-term care facilities, investigates complaints, and ensures quality care and rights protection. Run by SC Department on Aging.
programs.who_its_for: Residents of nursing homes, assisted living, and similar facilities in SC.
programs.benefit_value: Free advocacy resolving ~80% complaints without litigation.

programs.name: State Property Tax Assistance (Homestead Exemption)
programs.what_it_does: This program exempts the first $50,000 of a property's fair market value from local property taxes. It provides significant relief on property tax bills for eligible homeowners.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Individuals who are 65 or older, totally disabled, or legally blind, and have lived in South Carolina for at least one year.[2]
programs.whats_unique: This is a state-specific homestead exemption offering a fixed $50,000 exemption amount, which goes beyond standard federal programs and varies by state in generosity and structure.[2]
programs.benefit_value: Exemption on first $50,000 of fair market value from property taxes.[2]

programs.name: Senior Farmers Market Nutrition Value Program
programs.what_it_does: This program provides $25 worth of coupons for eligible seniors to purchase fresh, locally grown fruits and vegetables at participating farmers markets from June until October 15. It supplements the diet of low-income seniors with nutritious produce while supporting South Carolina's small farmers.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors in the Pee Dee Region, issued on a first-come, first-served basis until supply is exhausted; over 5,000 individuals served annually.[1]
programs.whats_unique: This is a state-funded voucher program specifically collaborative between the Department of Social Services and CareSouth Carolina, targeted at regional low-income seniors to boost local agriculture—not a universal federal initiative.[1]

programs.name: Vantage Point
programs.what_it_does: Vantage Point serves as the first place for accurate, unbiased information on all aspects of aging or living with a disability, offering resources like adult day centers, in-home care, support groups, counseling, caregiver training, and limited financial assistance for household modifications, respite, supplies, and nutrition. It is the only community health center in the nation designated as an Area Agency on Aging and Aging Disability Resource Center by South Carolina, the Administration on Aging, and CMS.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Older adults and individuals living with disabilities seeking information, support, and service linkages.[1]
programs.whats_unique: Unique national designation as a combined community health center, Area Agency on Aging, and ADRC, providing integrated one-stop services specific to South Carolina's structure.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Limited financial assistance for modifications, respite, supplies, nutrition; serves thousands via comprehensive support.[1]

programs.name: Senior Medicare Fraud Patrol
programs.what_it_does: This collaborative program between the Lieutenant Governor’s Office on Aging and CareSouth Carolina increases public awareness, reduces Medicare fraud incidence, encourages self-monitoring and reporting, and promotes volunteer participation. It helps eliminate fraudulent practices targeting seniors.[1]
programs.who_its_for: South Carolina seniors at risk of or affected by Medicare fraud.[1]
programs.whats_unique: State-specific collaborative initiative focused on Medicare fraud prevention through education, reporting, and volunteers, not replicated as a named program elsewhere.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Fraud prevention, awareness, and reporting support; indirect financial protection via reduced fraud losses.[1]
